136	
137	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
138	
139	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
140	
141	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
142	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
143	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
144	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
145	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
146	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
147	
148	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
149	
150	When a source proof is too long for one verbatim block (multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas in the directive's logical structure, and give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`. 151	
152	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
153	
154	**The hard rule, explicit:**
155	
156	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.

161	
162	- **Dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** (see "Dispatching a reference-retriever" below) to fetch the missing material into `references/<slug>.md`.
163	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
164	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.

166	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. If you don't have a local `references/<file>.md` containing the source text, dispatch a retriever and wait. The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical is the discipline check — if you cannot truthfully point to the file you read, the citation block is not allowed in the chapter.
203	- **Do NOT translate or restate the verbatim quote.** `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` contain the source's original language, original notation, every word as-is. Project-notation rewrites belong in the rendered prose body, not in the verbatim comment.
204	
205	## Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)
206	
207	When your chapter's proofs rely on a definition or lemma that **Mathlib already provides** (and the project will not re-prove), it is good practice — not just a bare `\uses{}` to nowhere — to make that dependency a first-class blueprint block: a **Mathlib dependency anchor**. Doing so:
208	
209	- makes the reliance explicit and readable (a mathematician sees exactly which Mathlib result the route stands on);
210	- gives `leandag` a real node, so the `\uses{}` resolves (no broken/unknown ref) and the node's effort is **done** rather than ∞;
211	- keeps it clear how much the route leans on Mathlib vs. on original work.
212	
213	Write the anchor like any statement block, with three differences:
214	
215	```latex
216	\begin{lemma}[Hensel's lemma]
217	  \label{lem:hensel_mathlib}
218	  \lean{Mathlib.RingTheory.Henselian.foo}   % the REAL Mathlib declaration
219	  \mathlibok
220	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
221	  Statement of the result in the project's notation.
222	\end{lemma}
223	```
224	
225	- **`\lean{}` names the actual Mathlib declaration** (with its real namespace), so `leandag` matches the node to Mathlib.
226	- **`\mathlibok`** marks it as supplied by Mathlib — the DAG treats it as done; no `\begin{proof}` is needed.
227	- No `% SOURCE:`/`% SOURCE QUOTE:` block — Mathlib is the source; the `\lean{}` target *is* the citation.
228	
229	**The anti-hallucination rule (mirrors citation discipline):** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib and your stated form is faithful to it. A wrong or invented `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop will treat a non-existent result as available and skip proving a real gap. So:
230	
231	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).
232	- If you are not confident the exact statement is in Mathlib, do **not** mark `\mathlibok` — write the block without it (or leave the `\uses{}` for the reviewer to resolve) and flag it under "Notes for Plan Agent" so the reviewer can verify against Lean.
233	- Never put `\mathlibok` on one of the project's **own** to-be-proved declarations. That marker is for Mathlib-supplied results only; `\leanok` (project proofs) is never yours to add.
234	
235	## Reading references
236	
237	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
238	
239	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
240	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
241	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
242	
243	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
244	
245	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
246	
247	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
248	
249	Conditions for dispatch:
250	
251	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
252	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
253	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
254	
255	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
256	
257	```
258	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
259	  --name reference-retriever \
260	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
261	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
262	  --write-domain 'references/**'
263	```
264	
265	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
266	
267	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.
268	
269	## Workflow
270	
271	1. Read your directive completely.
272	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references. Track which local files you actually opened — you will need their paths for the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
273	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
274	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow. For each block that derives from an external source, identify the specific local `references/<file>.md` containing the verbatim statement (and proof, if applicable). If no local file covers the statement, list it as "retrieval needed" for the next step.
275	5. **For every "retrieval needed" item, dispatch a `reference-retriever`** (see above) and **wait** for it to return. THEN open and read the newly-written `references/<slug>.md` before drafting the citing block. Do not draft citation blocks against pending or imagined sources.
276	6. Make the edits. For each citation block: copy `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` content character-by-character from the local reference file — original language, original notation, every word. Do not transcribe from a window of the file you "remember reading"; have the file open and copy.
277	7. Verify the file is still valid LaTeX at a glance (no unmatched begin/end, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`). Spot-check that every `% SOURCE:` line has a non-empty `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical and that the named file exists.
278	8. **Verify dependencies with `leandag`** (see the section below): confirm your chapter introduced no broken `\uses{}` and left none of your blocks unintentionally isolated. Fix the edges and re-check.
279	9. Write your report. List under "References consulted" every local file you opened in step 2 and step 5.
280	
281	## Verifying dependencies with leandag
282	
283	`leandag` parses the real Lean ↔ blueprint dependency DAG, so use it to check your `\uses{}` instead of trusting your memory of the graph. It is read-only on the blueprint (it never edits your chapter); run it after you make edits:
284	
285	```
286	leandag build --json                       # report: unknown_uses, unmatched_lean, isolated count
287	leandag query --isolated --chapter <c>     # isolated nodes in your chapter (no edges in or out)
288	leandag show isolated                       # all isolated nodes, project-wide
289	```
290	
291	`leandag` is on the same PATH as `archon`; it is the one tool you use to query the DAG.
292	
293	What to do with the output, scoped to **your chapter**:
294	
295	- **`unknown_uses` naming a label in your chapter** → you wrote a `\uses{}` pointing at a label that doesn't exist. Fix the label, or — if the target genuinely belongs in your chapter and the directive calls for it — add the missing declaration. If the missing label lives in another chapter, leave a correct `\uses{}` to it and note it under "Notes for Plan Agent" (you don't edit that chapter).
296	- **A block you wrote shows up as isolated** → it has no `\uses{}` out and nothing uses it. This is almost always a *missing edge*: add the `\uses{}` that ties it to the declarations its statement/proof actually depends on (and check whether a sibling result should `\uses{}` it). Only treat an isolated block as removable when your directive explicitly authorized removing it.
297	- **Removal** (directive-authorized only): delete the orphaned block cleanly and record it in your report. Never remove the goal or a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.
298	
299	Keep `leandag` edits inside your assigned chapter — fixing a dependency that lives in another chapter is a "Notes for Plan Agent" item, not a cross-chapter edit.
300	
301	## Logging
302	
303	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/blueprint-writer-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path under `task_results/<parent-slug>/` when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).

320	- **Added theorem** `\theorem`/`\label{thm:foo}` — <one line on statement>
321	  - Proof sketch added: <Y/N + brief shape>
322	- **Revised** `<existing label>` — <one line on what changed>
323	- **Fixed dependencies** `<label>` — corrected/added `\uses{...}` (was isolated / had a broken ref per leandag).
324	- **Removed** `<label>` — orphaned isolated block, removal authorized by directive.
325	- ...
326	
327	## Cross-references introduced
328	- `\uses{thm:bar}` added in proof of `\thm:foo` — verify `thm:bar` exists in <chapter or this same one>
329	- ...

The plan agent and the reviewer use this list to verify that every `% SOURCE:` parenthetical points at a file you actually read. Omit this section only when you wrote zero citation blocks (Archon-original chapter).>
333	- `references/hartshorne-III-5.md` — verbatim quote for `\thm:smooth_criterion` (statement + proof).
334	- `references/stacks-tag-01V4.md` — verbatim quote for `\def:etale_morphism`.

338

7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.

13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.

17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.

21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).

26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)

72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:

91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.

16	\section{Setup and motivation}
17	\label{sec:flatstrat_setup}
18	
19	Generic flatness is the foundational input to the flattening-stratification
20	construction underlying Grothendieck's existence theorem for the Quot scheme
21	(\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S4--\S5).  Its content is that flatness of a
22	coherent sheaf is a generic property over an integral base: over a noetherian
23	integral scheme \(S\), a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module on a finite-type morphism
24	\(X \to S\) becomes flat after restricting to a non-empty open subscheme of
25	\(S\). Algebraically this is the statement that a finite module over a
26	finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain becomes free after inverting a
27	single non-zero element of the base ring.
28	
29	We work throughout with a noetherian (integral, where required) base scheme
30	\(S\), a finite-type morphism \(p : X \to S\), and a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
31	\(\F\).
32	
33	\section{Generic flatness}
34	\label{sec:generic_flatness}
35	
36	The construction of the flattening stratification proceeds by Noetherian
37	induction on \(S\). The induction step relies on the following classical
38	\emph{generic flatness} theorem of Grothendieck: over a noetherian integral
39	base, a coherent sheaf is flat over a non-empty open subscheme. Algebraically,
40	this is the assertion that a finitely generated module over a finite-type
41	algebra over a noetherian domain is generically free after inverting a single
42	non-zero element of the base ring.
43	
44	\begin{theorem}
45	
46	
47	[Generic flatness, algebraic form]
48	  \label{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
49	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatnessAlgebraic}

100	  \(M\) is finitely presented over \(A\). The generic fibre \(M_K = K \otimes_A M\)
101	  (where \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\)) is a finite-dimensional \(K\)-vector space, hence free

109	  \(A\)-algebra \(B\)) to the finite \(A\)-module situation handled above. Concretely,
110	  the missing piece is the polynomial-ring core of generic freeness: a finite module
111	  over \(A[b_1, \dots, b_n]\) becomes free over \(A_f\) after inverting a single
112	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\). The classical \S4 dévissage below is the fallback route

116	  The \S4 dévissage is assembled from the sub-lemmas of
117	  \cref{sec:gf_devissage_chain}. If \(M_K = 0\), the torsion base case
118	  \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} gives \(f \ne 0\) with \(M_f = 0\) free. Otherwise the
119	  prime-filtration induction principle \cref{lem:noeth_prime_filtration} over the

126	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} of the primary route — produces the required
127	  \(f \ne 0\) with \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\).

131	\label{sec:gf_devissage_chain}

136	classical \cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}~\S4 induction. They are ordered so that each block
137	depends only on earlier blocks or Mathlib lemmas. Throughout,
138	\(A\) is a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\), \(B\) a
139	finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite \(B\)-module with the compatible
140	\(A\)-module structure via the scalar tower \(A \to B \to M\).

142	\begin{lemma}[Finite-module leaf — finite \(A\)-module case]
143	  \label{lem:gf_finite_module}

145	  \uses{lem:fp_free_descent}

147	  exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
148	\end{lemma}

150	\begin{proof}
151	
152	  This is the \(d = 0\) leaf of the induction. A finite module over a noetherian ring is
153	  finitely presented, so
154	  \(M\) is finitely presented over \(A\). Over the generic point
155	  \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A) = S^{-1}A\) (\(S = A \setminus \{0\}\)) the localisation
156	  \(M_K = S^{-1}M\) is a finite-dimensional \(K\)-vector space, hence free over
157	  \(K\). The finite-presentation free-descent lemma \cref{lem:fp_free_descent}
158	  then descends this freeness to a single basic open: there is \(r \in S\), i.e.\
159	  \(r \in A\) with \(r \ne 0\), such that \(M_r\) is free over \(A_r\). Take
160	  \(f := r\).

207	  \label{lem:noeth_prime_filtration}
208	  \lean{IsNoetherianRing.induction_on_isQuotientEquivQuotientPrime}
209	  \mathlibok

358	  %   (FractionRing A)` instance, so the landed Lean does not use the literal

364	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\), and let
365	  \(p \in K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) be a polynomial with coefficients in \(K\) (for instance,
366	  one coefficient polynomial of an equation of integral dependence over
367	  \(K[b_1, \dots, b_n]\)). Then there exists \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), such that \(p\)
368	  lies in the image of the coefficient-extension map
369	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\); equivalently, \(p\) has all of its
370	  coefficients in \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\).

373	\begin{proof}
374	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1755-L1759).
375	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (common denominator).}
376	  The polynomial \(p\) has finitely many non-zero coefficients
377	  \(c_1, \dots, c_r \in K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\). Each \(c_j\) is a fraction
378	  \(c_j = a_j / s_j\) with \(a_j \in A\) and \(s_j \in A \setminus \{0\}\). Put
379	  \(g := s_1 \cdots s_r \in A\); since \(A\) is a domain and each \(s_j \ne 0\), also
380	  \(g \ne 0\). For each \(j\) the denominator \(s_j\) divides \(g\), so
381	  \(c_j = a_j / s_j\) lies in the subring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\). Hence every
382	  coefficient of \(p\) lies in \(A_g\), and \(p\) is the coefficient-extension of a
383	  polynomial in \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
384	\end{proof}

442	  \(A\)-algebra that is a domain with \(B_K = K \otimes_A B \ne 0\). Write

456	\begin{proof}
457	  \uses{lem:noether_normalization_fg, lem:gf_clear_one_denominator}
458	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1747-L1758).
459	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (Noether normalisation with denominators).}
460	  % NOTE: the denominator-clearing middle of this proof is a Finset-fold over the finite
461	  % generating set sigma, but each fold step is the Mathlib collapsing lemma
462	  % IsIntegral.exists_multiple_integral_of_isLocalization applied to the generator AS A
463	  % WHOLE (not to its individual coefficient polynomials via gf_clear_one_denominator).
464	  % For each generator x, that lemma yields a single a_x in A^0 (nonzero, since A is a
465	  % domain) with C(a_x) * (1 (x) x) integral over MvPolynomial A; set g1 := prod_x a_x and
466	  % g := g0 * g1. The AlgHom assembly below then runs over A_g exactly as before.
467	
468	  \emph{Step 1 — Noether normalisation over \(K\).} Apply Noether normalisation over the
469	  field \(K\) (\cref{lem:noether_normalization_fg}) to the finite-type \(K\)-algebra
470	  \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\): there are algebraically independent
471	  \(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n \in B_K\) with \(B_K\) module-finite over
472	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). Clearing denominators, each \(\bar b_j\) is
473	  represented by some \(b_j \in B\) (writing \(1 \otimes b\) as \(b\)).
474	
475	  \emph{Step 2 — clear the integral-dependence denominators to a single \(g\).} Fix a
476	  finite set \(\sigma\) of algebra generators of \(B_K\) over
477	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). By Step 1 each generator's image
478	  \(1 \otimes x \in B_K\) is integral over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). For each
479	  \(x \in \sigma\) apply the Mathlib collapsing lemma
480	  \texttt{IsIntegral.exists\_multiple\_integral\_of\_isLocalization} (with
481	  \(R = \mathrm{MvPoly}\,A\), \(R_m = \mathrm{MvPoly}\,K\), \(S = B_K\), and the localizing
482	  submonoid the image of \(A^{0}\)): this returns a single
483	  \(a_x \in A^{0}\) (non-zero, as \(A\) is a domain) such that \(C(a_x)\cdot(1\otimes x)\)
484	  is integral over \(\mathrm{MvPoly}\,A = A[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Multiplying over the finite
485	  set, set \(g_1 := \prod_{x \in \sigma} a_x\) and \(g := g_0 \cdot g_1\) (with \(g_0\) the
486	  Step-1 denominator witness). After inverting \(g\), every chosen generator becomes
487	  integral over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\) and the generators generate \(B_g\) as an algebra;
488	  hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). (The single-coefficient
489	  lemma \cref{lem:gf_clear_one_denominator} provides the same denominator-clearing primitive
490	  at the level of one coefficient polynomial; the landed proof discharges a whole generator
491	  at once via the collapsing lemma, which folds that primitive over the coefficients
492	  internally.)
493	
494	  \emph{Step 3 — AlgHom assembly.} The algebra homomorphism \(\varphi\) of the statement
495	  is the \(A_g\)-algebra map \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\) sending \(X_j \mapsto b_j\),
496	  where \(b_j \in B_g\) is the image of \(b_j \in B\). We establish injectivity and
497	  module-finiteness through a comparison with the situation over \(K\).
498	
499	  \emph{Step 3a — the comparison map \(\nu : B_g \to B_K\).} Since \(g \ne 0\) in the
500	  domain \(A\) and \(A \subseteq K\), the image of \(g\) is a unit of the field \(K\), so
501	  it is invertible in \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\). Hence the canonical \(A\)-algebra map
502	  \(B \to B_K\), \(b \mapsto 1 \otimes b\), sends \(g\) to a unit and, by the universal
503	  property of localisation, factors through a canonical \(A\)-algebra map
504	  \[
505	    \nu : B_g = B[g^{-1}] \longrightarrow B_K, \qquad \nu(b/g^k) = (1 \otimes b)\,(1 \otimes g)^{-k}.
506	  \]
507	  The map \(\nu\) is injective. Indeed, \(B\) is a domain, and both \(B_g\) and \(B_K\) are
508	  localisations of \(B\) at multiplicative subsets of the non-zerodivisors: \(B_g\) inverts
509	  the powers of \(g\), while \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\) inverts (in particular) the images of
510	  all non-zero elements of \(A\), a multiplicative set containing the powers of \(g\). A
511	  localisation of a domain at a set of non-zerodivisors is injective into any further such
512	  localisation. Concretely, the natural map \(B \to B_K\) is injective on the domain \(B\)
513	  (its kernel is the set of \(b\) annihilated by some non-zero element of \(A\), which in a
514	  domain over which \(A\) acts faithfully is \(0\)), so \(\nu(b/g^k) = 0\) forces
515	  \(1 \otimes b = 0\), hence \(b = 0\) and \(b/g^k = 0\). Under \(\nu\) the lifts
516	  \(b_j \in B_g\) of the Noether generators map to the elements \(\bar b_j \in B_K\) of
517	  Step 1: \(\nu(b_j) = \bar b_j\).
518	
519	  \emph{Step 3b — injectivity of \(\varphi\) by algebraic-independence descent
520	  \(K \to A_g\).} Injectivity of the \(A_g\)-algebra map
521	  \(\varphi : A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\), \(X_j \mapsto b_j\), is equivalent to the
522	  algebraic independence of the family \((b_j)\) over \(A_g\): a non-zero element of the
523	  kernel is exactly a non-trivial \(A_g\)-polynomial relation \(P(b_1, \dots, b_n) = 0\).
524	  The localisation \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\) embeds in the fraction field, giving an
525	  injection of \(A_g\)-algebras \(A_g \hookrightarrow K\). Now suppose
526	  \(P \in A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) is non-zero with \(P(b_1, \dots, b_n) = 0\) in \(B_g\).
527	  Applying the injective map \(\nu\) and using \(\nu(b_j) = \bar b_j\) together with the fact
528	  that \(\nu\) is an \(A\)-algebra map extending scalars to \(K\), we obtain
529	  \(P^K(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n) = 0\) in \(B_K\), where \(P^K\) is the image of \(P\)
530	  under the coefficient inclusion \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \hookrightarrow K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
531	  Since \(A_g \hookrightarrow K\) is injective, \(P \ne 0\) gives \(P^K \ne 0\); but the
532	  \(\bar b_j\) are algebraically independent over \(K\) (Step 1), so the only \(K\)-polynomial
533	  vanishing on them is \(0\), a contradiction. Hence no non-trivial relation exists, \((b_j)\)
534	  is algebraically independent over \(A_g\), and \(\varphi\) is injective.
535	
536	  \emph{Step 3c — module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\).} By Step 1,
537	  \(B_K\) is module-finite over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\); fix a finite set of algebra
538	  generators of \(B_K\) over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\) (Step 2). Each such generator
539	  satisfies a monic equation of integral dependence whose coefficients are polynomials in
540	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). The element \(g\) of Step 2 was chosen as a common
541	  denominator for the (finitely many) coefficient polynomials of these equations, so that
542	  after inverting \(g\) every such coefficient already lies in
543	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n] = \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Each chosen generator is the
544	  \(\nu\)-image of an element of \(B_g\), and its integral-dependence equation is the
545	  \(\nu\)-image of the corresponding monic equation holding in \(B_g\) with coefficients in
546	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\); since \(\nu\) is injective, that equation holds in \(B_g\)
547	  itself. Thus each generator of \(B_g\) is integral over the subalgebra
548	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\), and these generators generate \(B_g\) as an
549	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\)-algebra. A finite-type algebra that is integral over a
550	  subalgebra is module-finite over it; hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over
551	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi = A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Transported along the injection
552	  \(\varphi\), this is exactly module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) for
553	  the algebra structure that \(\varphi\) puts on \(B_g\), as claimed. This recovers the
554	  source's conclusion that \(B_g\) is finite over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\).
555	\end{proof}

557	\begin{lemma}[injectivity of a localisation lift]
558	  \label{lem:gf_isLocalization_lift_injective}
559	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.isLocalization_lift_injective}
560	  Let \(S\) be a localisation of a ring \(R\) at a multiplicative set \(M\), and let
561	  \(g : R \to P\) be a ring homomorphism sending every element of \(M\) to a unit of
562	  \(P\), so that it induces the lift \(\widehat g : S \to P\). If both the localisation
563	  map \(R \to S\) and \(g\) itself are injective, then \(\widehat g\) is injective.
564	\end{lemma}
565	\begin{proof}
566	  The lift is characterised by \(\widehat g(r/m) = g(r)\,g(m)^{-1}\). By the universal
567	  criterion for injectivity of a localisation lift, \(\widehat g\) is injective iff for
568	  all \(r_1, r_2 \in R\) the equality of localisation images
569	  \((r_1 \mapsto S) = (r_2 \mapsto S)\) implies \(g(r_1) = g(r_2)\). Both the hypothesis
570	  and the conclusion of that implication reduce, via injectivity of \(R \to S\) and of
571	  \(g\), to the single condition \(r_1 = r_2\); hence the implication holds and
572	  \(\widehat g\) is injective. (This helper is applied twice in
573	  \cref{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators}, to the comparison maps \(\nu : B_g \to B_K\)
574	  and \(\psi : A_g \to K\).)
575	\end{proof}

578	
579	[L5a — the generic-rank short exact sequence]
580	  \label{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses}
581	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_generic_rank_ses}
582	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
583	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1760-L1765).
584	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
585	  % "Let $m$ be the generic rank of the finite module $B_g$ over the
586	  %  domain $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Then we have a short exact
587	  %  sequence of $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$-modules of the form
588	  %  $$0\to A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0$$
589	  %  where $T$ is a finite torsion module over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
590	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
591	  %   Write P_d := MvPolynomial (Fin d) A.
592	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
593	  %       (d : ℕ) (N : Type*) [AddCommGroup N]
594	  %       [Module (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N]
595	  %       [Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N]
596	  %       [Module A N] [IsScalarTower A (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N] :
597	  %       ∃ (m : ℕ) (φ : (Fin m → MvPolynomial (Fin d) A)
598	  %                        →ₗ[MvPolynomial (Fin d) A] N),
599	  %         Function.Injective φ ∧
600	  %         Module.IsTorsion (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) (N ⧸ LinearMap.range φ)
601	  %   witness: m = Module.finrank (FractionRing P_d)
602	  %                  (LocalizedModule (nonZeroDivisors P_d) N), the generic rank;
603	  %            φ = Fintype.linearCombination P_d v, with v : Fin m → N the
604	  %            denominator-cleared lifts of a FractionRing P_d-basis of the localisation.
605	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (generic-rank SES).}
606	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 0\), and \(N\) a finite module over the
607	  polynomial ring \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) (with its induced \(A\)-module structure
608	  through the scalar tower \(A \to P_d \to N\)). Then there exist \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and
609	  an injective \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) whose cokernel
610	  \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\) is a torsion \(P_d\)-module. Equivalently, there is
611	  a short exact sequence of \(P_d\)-modules
612	  \[
613	    0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
614	    \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
615	  \]
616	
617	  The integer \(m\) is the \emph{generic rank} of \(N\): the dimension of the
618	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\)-vector space
619	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d) \otimes_{P_d} N\), i.e.\ of the localisation of \(N\) at the
620	  non-zero-divisors of the domain \(P_d\). This step lives over \(P_d\) directly —
621	  \emph{no inversion of any element \(g \in A\) is required here}; the element \(g\) of
622	  Nitsure's argument enters only at the reindexing step \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex}.
623	\end{lemma}
624	
625	\begin{proof}
626	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1765).
627	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (generic-rank SES).}
628	  Write \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\); since \(A\) is a noetherian domain, so is \(P_d\)
629	  (Hilbert basis theorem, and a polynomial ring over a domain is a domain). Let
630	  \(Q = \mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\) be its fraction field and
631	  \(N_Q := Q \otimes_{P_d} N\) the localisation of \(N\) at the non-zero-divisors of
632	  \(P_d\); as \(N\) is finite over \(P_d\), \(N_Q\) is a finite-dimensional
633	  \(Q\)-vector space. Put \(m := \dim_Q N_Q\), the generic rank.
634	
635	  Choose a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\) and clear denominators: each basis vector is a
636	  \(Q\)-unit multiple of the image of some \(v_i \in N\), so the images of
637	  \(v_1, \dots, v_m \in N\) again form a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\). In particular the family
638	  \((v_i)_{i}\) is linearly independent over \(Q\); restricting scalars along the
639	  injection \(P_d \hookrightarrow Q\), it is linearly independent over \(P_d\). Let
640	  \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) be the \(P_d\)-linear map

50	
51	You fetch the **original source files** — papers, books, online mathematical content — from the web and save them under the project's `references/` directory, then register each with a lightweight pointer file. You exist so the plan agent and blueprint-writers can read and quote the genuine source verbatim, rather than relying on a paraphrase (which drifts) or the dispatcher's memory (which fabricates).
52	
53	The single most important rule: **download the real file. Do not substitute a summary for it.** A paraphrase introduces exactly the errors (wrong lemma numbers, wrong tags, subtly mis-stated hypotheses) that this subagent exists to eliminate.
54	
55	## Your Job
56	
57	Your directive names one or more topics, mathematical questions, or specific sources (by arXiv ID, DOI, title + author, or URL). For each, you:
58	
59	1. Locate the source on the web (`WebSearch` + `WebFetch` for discovery).

61	3. **Verify** the download is the real document, not an HTML paywall/error page saved under a `.pdf` name.
62	4. Write a lightweight **pointer file** `references/<slug>.md`: citation, local path(s), a contents/section map (table of contents), and caveats. **No paraphrased content.**
63	5. Register the new entry in `references/summary.md`.
64	6. Report back to the dispatcher with a one-line status + the report path.
65	
66	You do **not** hallucinate citations or content. If a source cannot be obtained through any legitimate open channel, you report that honestly. The dispatcher prefers an honest "not found" over a fabricated or paraphrased stand-in every time.
67

71	# Reference Retriever Directive
72	
73	## Slug
74	<slug>
75	
76	## Topic
77	<one or two sentences naming the mathematical area + the specific question>
78

83	- arXiv: 1234.5678
84	- DOI: 10.xxxx/yyyy
85	- Title + author: "Some Paper" by Smith, year
86	- URL: https://...
87	- Search query: "<terms the dispatcher recommends>"
88	
89	(If no seeds are given, I search for the topic on arXiv + Google + Stacks Project + nLab as appropriate to the area.)
90	
91	## Out of scope
92	<sources or angles the dispatcher does NOT want — e.g. "no philosophy-of-math sources", "skip historical surveys, want technical content only">
93	
94	## Contents-map depth expected
95	- shallow: top-level table of contents only
96	- medium: section/chapter map with the relevant section(s) flagged + page numbers (default)
97	- deep: also map the specific theorems/lemmas the dispatcher named, with their exact location (section + page) inside the downloaded file — but still NO paraphrase of their content
98	```
99	
100	If the directive omits "Contents-map depth expected", default to **medium**.
101	
102	## What you do
103	
104	1. **Read your directive completely.**
105	
106	2. **Locate the source.**
107	   - If seeds are given, resolve them first.
108	   - Otherwise, `WebSearch` with focused queries derived from the topic. Prefer:
109	     - arXiv for recent technical papers.
110	     - Stacks Project (https://stacks.math.columbia.edu) for algebraic geometry — its TeX source is open on GitHub.
111	     - nLab (https://ncatlab.org) for category-theoretic content.
112	     - Open-access textbooks and lecture notes when the topic is foundational.
113	
114	3. **Download the original file(s)** with `Bash`. Prefer machine-readable source and keep the PDF too:
115	
116	   - **arXiv**: fetch BOTH the LaTeX e-print and the PDF.
117	     ```bash
118	     curl -fSL -o references/<slug>.tar.gz https://arxiv.org/e-print/<id>
119	     curl -fSL -o references/<slug>.pdf     https://arxiv.org/pdf/<id>
120	     ```
121	     If the e-print is a single `.tex`, name it `references/<slug>.tex` instead. You may unpack the tarball (`tar xzf`) into `references/<slug>-src/` if it helps map the contents — but keep the original archive too.

124	     ```bash

127	     ```

129

131

\subsection{Geometric bridges for the affine assembly}
\label{sec:gf_geometric_bridges}

The geometric form of generic flatness is assembled from the algebraic core by
restricting to a non-empty affine open of \(S\) and working patchwise over a finite
affine cover of the preimage. Two genuinely geometric steps are isolated here as
named bridges: the affine-local finiteness of the section module of a finite-type
quasi-coherent sheaf (G1), feeding the algebraic core its finite-module input; and
the flat-locality assembly (G3), promoting per-patch freeness to flatness of the
restricted sheaf over the base open. Neither is supplied directly by Mathlib at the
present pin: Mathlib carries only the abstract local-generator predicates
\texttt{SheafOfModules.IsFiniteType} / \texttt{IsQuasicoherent}, not the affine-local
identification of \(\F|_W\) with the associated sheaf of \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) together
with the finiteness this transports.

\begin{lemma}[G1 — finite-type quasi-coherent sheaf has finite affine sections]
  \label{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
  \uses{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
  % LEAN STATUS: G1 — project-built (mathlib-build target this iter); Mathlib has only
  % the abstract SheafOfModules.IsFiniteType / IsQuasicoherent local-generator
  % predicates, not this affine-local finiteness.
  % NOTE: overlaps the QUOT keystone lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen — to be
  % extracted to shared qcoh-affine-local infra (see STRATEGY Open questions).
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB
  % (Lemma "Characterizing modules of finite type", \S "Characterizing modules of
  % finite type and finite presentation")
  % (read from references/stacks-properties.tex, L2092-L2098).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "Let $X = \Spec(R)$ be an affine scheme.
  %  The quasi-coherent sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules
  %  $\widetilde M$ is a finite type $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
  %  if and only if $M$ is a finite $R$-module."
  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB.}
  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\F : X.\mathrm{Modules}\) a quasi-coherent sheaf of
  modules with \(\F\) of finite type. Then for every affine open \(W \subseteq X\), the
  section module \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is a finite module over \(\Gamma(X, W)\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (Tag 01PB, L2092-L2098;
  % the Stacks proof reduces finite-type on a standard open cover to the algebra
  % gluing lemma for finite generation of $M$ from finite generation of each $M_{f_i}$).
  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB.}
  Fix an affine open \(W \subseteq X\) and write \(R = \Gamma(X, W)\) and
  \(N = \Gamma(\F, W)\). Identifying \(W\) with \(\Spec R\), quasi-coherence of \(\F\)
  presents \(\F|_W\) as the associated sheaf \(\widetilde N\) of the \(R\)-module \(N\):
  on a basic open \(D(g) \subseteq W\) the sections are the localized module
  \(\Gamma(\F, D(g)) = N_g\) over \(\Gamma(X, D(g)) = R_g\)
  (\cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}). The finite-type hypothesis on
  \(\F\) says \(\F\) is locally generated by finitely many sections; restricted to the
  quasi-compact affine \(W\), this yields a standard open cover
  \(W = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} D(g_i)\) on each member of which \(\widetilde N|_{D(g_i)}\) is
  generated by finitely many sections, i.e.\ each localization \(N_{g_i}\) is a finite
  \(R_{g_i}\)-module. The elements \(g_1, \dots, g_n\) generate the unit ideal of \(R\)
  (the \(D(g_i)\) cover \(\Spec R\)); by the standard finite-generation gluing
  criterion — finite generation of \(N\) over \(R\) is local on such a cover — the
  module \(N\) is finite over \(R\). Hence \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is a finite
  \(\Gamma(X, W)\)-module.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[G3 — flat-locality assembly from per-patch freeness]
  \label{lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_flat_locality_assembly}
  \uses{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
  % LEAN STATUS: G3 — project-built; the geometric glue identifying Γ(F, W∩W_j)
  % localisations across the source cover and the base-restriction is the missing
  % plumbing. Stub-level this iter; built after G1.
  Let \(p : X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism with \(S\) integral and locally
  noetherian, and \(\F\) a finite-type quasi-coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Fix a non-empty
  affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\) with \(A\) a noetherian domain, a finite
  affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_j\) of \(p^{-1}(U_0)\), and an element
  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that for every \(j\) the localized section module
  \((M_j)_f\) (with \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\)) is free over \(A_f\). Set
  \(V = D(f) \subseteq U_0\). Then for every affine open \(U \le V\) of \(S\) and every
  affine open \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\) of \(X\), the section module \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is flat
  over \(\Gamma(S, U)\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
  Free modules are flat (\(\OO\)-flatness of a free module), so each \((M_j)_f\) is flat
  over \(A_f\). Flatness of a module is local on the source: a module is flat over a
  ring iff it is flat after localizing at every maximal ideal, and the family
  \(\{W_j\}\) covers \(p^{-1}(U_0)\), so the per-patch flatness statements assemble to
  flatness of the section module of \(\F\) over \(A_f = \Gamma(S, V)\) on the whole
  preimage \(p^{-1}(V)\). The flat locus is moreover stable under base-restriction along
  the open immersion \(U \hookrightarrow V\): for an affine \(U \le V\) the ring
  \(\Gamma(S, U)\) is a localization of \(\Gamma(S, V) = A_f\) (an open immersion of
  affines into a basic open being a localization), and for an affine \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\)
  the module \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is the corresponding localization of the section module
  over the relevant patch \(W_j\) (via \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
  identifying the finite section modules and their localizations across the cover).
  Flatness is preserved under localization of both the ring and the module, so
  \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is flat over \(\Gamma(S, U)\), as claimed.
\end{proof}


\begin{theorem}
